name: GOV.UK Building regulations approval
key_points_for_site: Building regulations approval is different from planning permission and a project may need both.
Some work does not require the homeowner to apply directly if a competent person scheme member is used.
England-first guidance should be labelled and not implied to cover Scotland, Wales or Northern Ireland without separate checks.
codex_handling: Use as the primary authority for ordinary homeowner building regulations approval route pages.

name: GOV.UK competent person scheme guidance
key_points_for_site: Competent person schemes allow registered tradespeople to self-certify certain work instead of the homeowner applying separately.
The site must tell users to check installer registration and keep the certificate/evidence.
codex_handling: Use for certificate checker, installer question pages and CPS download assets.

name: GOV.UK Approved Documents collection
key_points_for_site: Approved Documents give practical guidance on ways to meet building regulations in England.
They must be treated as guidance, not as the only lawful way to comply.
Pages must show document/version and warn users to re-check before starting work.
codex_handling: Use for Approved Document hubs and source/version blocks.

name: GOV.UK Approved Document L 2026
key_points_for_site: Approved Document L 2026 was published on 24 March 2026.
Earlier versions can continue to apply to buildings subject to previous regulatory standards.
The PDF/source notes include staged commencement dates, so energy pages must be version-aware.
codex_handling: Use only with visible version warnings and transition handling.

name: Future Homes and Buildings Standards: Building Circular 01/2026
key_points_for_site: Circular 01/2026 explains updates connected with future homes/buildings standards.
Codex must not collapse these changes into older Part L or Part F pages without transition notes.
codex_handling: Use for energy/ventilation version notes and update queue.

name: GOV.UK Building control approval for higher-risk buildings
key_points_for_site: BSR oversees and approves building work for higher-risk buildings in England.
The guidance defines HRB thresholds around 7 storeys or 18 metres and building use/residential units.
Ordinary homeowner tools must route HRB cases out to specialist/BSR guidance rather than giving simplified advice.
codex_handling: Use as a stop-route and warning source, not as a normal DIY/homeowner flow.
